era_context: Early 17th century: The Thirty Years' War is in its early phases; the Tokugawa shogunate is consolidating power in Japan through military campaigns; the Ming Dynasty continues in China; the early Stuart period in England; the Dutch Golden Age is expanding; the Safavid Empire controls Persia and the Levant.

events.title: Siege of Osaka (main phase)
events.description: The Siege of Osaka, lasting from November 1614 to May 1615, was the final major military conflict of Japan's Sengoku period. Tokugawa Ieyasu and his son Tokugawa Hidetada besieged Osaka Castle, held by the Toyotomi clan (Toyotomi Hideyori and his mother Yodo-dono). The siege involved massive forces, innovative siege warfare, and the use of artillery. The Toyotomi were defeated, their castle destroyed, and their clan effectively eliminated as a political power. This victory secured Tokugawa hegemony and ushered in the Edo period of relative peace and stability lasting over 250 years.

sources.name: Tokugawa family records and administrative documents
Archaeological evidence from Osaka Castle excavations

events.certainty_note: The siege is extensively documented in Japanese chronicles compiled within a generation; archaeological evidence confirms the destruction layer.

events.title: Edict of Nantes revoked (preparation and context)
events.description: In 1615, tensions between Catholic and Protestant factions in France were escalating, setting the stage for the revocation of the Edict of Nantes in 1685. The Edict of Nantes (1598) had granted religious toleration to Huguenots (French Protestants). By 1615, under the regency of Marie de Medici (Louis XIII was a minor), Catholic influence was strengthening. Religious conflicts, including the Huguenot rebellions of 1621–1629, would dominate French politics for the next several decades. The 1615 context reflects the fragility of the 1598 settlement.
events.certainty_note: The political context of 1615 is well-documented; the causal link to the 1685 revocation is historiographic interpretation rather than direct evidence.

events.title: Matteo Ricci's death and legacy in China
events.description: Matteo Ricci, the Italian Jesuit missionary, died on 11 May 1610 (not 1615, but his influence was still dominant in 1615). However, in 1615, the Jesuit mission in China was consolidating Ricci's legacy under his successors, particularly Adam Schall von Bell. Ricci had introduced Western mathematics, astronomy, and cartography to the Ming court, earning respect and influence. By 1615, the Jesuit mission was well-established in Beijing and other major cities, representing one of the most successful Christian missionary efforts in East Asia. The Jesuits' scientific knowledge made them valuable to the Ming court.

sources.name: Ming court records (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Ricci died in 1610, not 1615; the 1615 context reflects the continuation and consolidation of his mission rather than a specific event in that year.

events.title: Pocahontas brought to England by English colonists
events.description: Pocahontas (Amonute), captured by English colonists in 1614 and married to John Rolfe in 1614, was brought to England in 1615 as a symbol of successful colonial 'civilisation' and conversion to Christianity. She was presented at court, met King James I, and became a celebrity in London. Her presence was intended to promote investment in the Virginia Company and demonstrate the feasibility of colonial settlement. However, the voyage and English climate took a severe toll on her health. She would die in March 1616 before returning to Virginia.

sources.name: John Smith's General History of Virginia (1624)
Contemporary English court records and correspondence

events.certainty_note: Pocahontas' arrival in England and presentation at court are documented in English sources; the date of 1615 is well-established.

events.title: Thirty Years' War: Palatinate Campaign and Battle of White Mountain aftermath
events.description: The Thirty Years' War (1618–1648) had not yet begun in 1615, but the political and religious tensions that would ignite it were acute. The Battle of White Mountain (1620) was still five years away. However, in 1615, the Palatinate was a flashpoint of religious and dynastic conflict. The Protestant Union and Catholic League were forming. The election of Frederick V as Elector Palatine in 1610 had intensified tensions. By 1615, the region was a powder keg awaiting the spark that would come in 1618.
events.certainty_note: The political context of 1615 is well-documented; the causal link to the 1618 war outbreak is historiographic interpretation.

events.title: Dutch-Portuguese naval conflict in the Indian Ocean
events.description: The Dutch East India Company (VOC) was engaged in ongoing naval and commercial conflicts with Portuguese traders and the Portuguese Estado da Índia throughout the early 17th century. In 1615, Dutch naval forces were consolidating control of the spice trade in the Moluccas and surrounding regions, displacing Portuguese merchants. These conflicts were part of the broader European struggle for dominance in Asian trade. The Dutch would eventually displace the Portuguese as the dominant European power in Southeast Asia by the mid-17th century.

sources.name: VOC records and Dutch East India Company archives

events.certainty_note: Dutch-Portuguese conflicts in this period are documented in company records; specific engagements in 1615 are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of the Siege of Osaka as a simple military victory obscures the political complexity: the Toyotomi were not merely military rivals but represented an alternative vision of Japanese political organisation. Modern scholarship emphasises the siege as a turning point toward centralised Tokugawa authoritarianism rather than a simple restoration of order. Additionally, the characterisation of Pocahontas as a willing cultural ambassador has been substantially revised; modern historians emphasise her captivity and lack of agency.
historiographic_note: 1615 is well-documented for European and East Asian history, with rich archival sources. The record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military narratives. Indigenous American and Southeast Asian perspectives are mediated through colonial sources. The focus on the Siege of Osaka and European religious conflicts may overshadow significant developments in other regions (Africa, South America, Central Asia) where documentation is sparser.

graph_edges.note: The Siege of Osaka (1615) led directly to the final phase and deaths of the Toyotomi in 1616.
The religious tensions of 1615 France eventually led to the revocation of the Edict of Nantes in 1685.
Pocahontas' arrival in England in 1615 led to her death in 1616.
The religious and political tensions of 1615 Central Europe led to the outbreak of the Thirty Years' War in 1618.
